Client: It’s basically Social Security. I think it’s like, 190-ish a year? That sounds high when I say it out loud.
Advisor: Yeah, that does sound unusually high for Social Security—just to check, are we mixing up annual versus monthly, or maybe there’s another source in there?
Client: Wait—oh. I might be mixing it up. I’m thinking of the annual number from the statement. It’s about $197,350 a year, gross.
Advisor: Got it—$197,350 gross annually. And that’s coming in as Social Security, right?
Client: Yes. Social Security is the only income right now.

Client: No, I’m not employed.
Advisor: Thanks. Now spending—about how much are your total monthly expenses, all-in? Mortgage or rent, groceries, insurance, kid stuff, everything.
Client: Around $7,800 a month. Maybe a touch more some months.
Advisor: When you say $7,800—do you mean that’s your spending after taxes, like what leaves the checking account, or is that a pre-tax budget number?
Client: After taxes. Like what actually goes out.

Client: Yes, please.
Advisor: Great. Income first—what’s your household gross income these days, roughly per year?
Client: It’s basically Social Security. I think it’s like, 190-ish a year? That sounds high when I say it out loud.
Advisor: Yeah, that does sound unusually high for Social Security—just to check, are we mixing up annual versus monthly, or maybe there’s another source in there?
Client: Wait—oh. I might be mixing it up. I’m thinking of the annual number from the statement. It’s about $197,350 a year, gross.

Advisor: Okay. Let me add those up in plain English: taxable about $996,650, retirement about $201,750, bank about $108,950, and private markets about $51,050.
Client: Yes, that sounds right.
Advisor: That totals about $1,358,400 in investable assets. Does that match what you see on your end?
Client: Yes—$1,358,400 is the total I’ve got written down.
Advisor: Great. Now, separate from investable assets—your home. What’s the approximate value of your primary residence?

Client: Yes—$1,358,400 is the total I’ve got written down.
Advisor: Great. Now, separate from investable assets—your home. What’s the approximate value of your primary residence?
Client: About $419,600.
Advisor: And any loan outstanding on the home—mortgage, HELOC—anything at all?
Client: No, it’s paid off. Zero loan.

Advisor: And country—just confirming you’re in the US, right?
Client: Yes, US.
Advisor: Okay. And marital status—earlier you mentioned being widowed. I just want to make sure I’m using the right term when we talk planning assumptions.
Client: Yes, I’m widowed.
Advisor: Thanks. I know that’s not a fun box to check.

Advisor: Okay. And going back to that point about feeling secure—let’s anchor on the basics first: income coming in, spending going out, then what you’ve got saved. Sound alright?
Client: Yes, please.
Advisor: Great. Income first—what’s your household gross income these days, roughly per year?
Client: It’s basically Social Security. I think it’s like, 190-ish a year? That sounds high when I say it out loud.
Advisor: Yeah, that does sound unusually high for Social Security—just to check, are we mixing up annual versus monthly, or maybe there’s another source in there?

Client: Yeah. It’s… still weird to say out loud sometimes.
Advisor: I hear you. We’ll keep it practical. Next one: risk tolerance. When markets swing, do you feel more conservative, more aggressive, or kind of in the middle?
Client: Middle. Moderate, I guess.
Advisor: When you say moderate—just to make sure we mean the same thing—does that mean you can handle some ups and downs for growth, but you don’t want anything that could drop a ton and keep you up at night?
Client: Exactly. Some fluctuation is okay, but I don’t want big surprises.

Advisor: Okay. Small tangent—are they home from school yet or still in class?
Client: Still in class, thank goodness.
Advisor: Fair. Alright—switching gears slightly. You’re working with me as a US RIA—registered investment advisor—does that match what you thought this relationship was?
Client: Uh, I thought you were just like a broker? Sorry—what’s the difference again?
Advisor: Totally fair question. A broker is more transaction-based; an RIA is advice-first and fiduciary. For our purposes here, I just need to confirm you’re in the US RIA channel—does that sound right for how you found us and how we’re working together?

Client: Yes, please.
Advisor: Great. Income first—what’s your household gross income these days, roughly per year?
Client: It’s basically Social Security. I think it’s like, 190-ish a year? That sounds high when I say it out loud.

Client: Wait—oh. I might be mixing it up. I’m thinking of the annual number from the statement. It’s about $197,350 a year, gross.

Client: Yes. That one is the biggest. It’s about $996,650.
Advisor: Okay—about $996,650 in the taxable account on the advisor platform. Any retirement accounts—401(k) or IRA type?
Client: Yes, I have a 401(k)/IRA bucket—around $201,750.
Advisor: Got it. And then cash—bank or money market—anything sitting more liquid?
Client: There’s about $108,950 in the bank side. It’s kind of my safety buffer.

Client: Yeah, seriously. Anyway—go ahead.
Advisor: Alright, Emily—thanks. Going back to that Social Security point for a second, because it ties into taxes: do you have a sense of what tax bracket you usually land in?
Client: I think we’re in the 35% bracket. I mean, I’m in the 35% bracket now.
Advisor: Got it—35%. And just so I’m not mixing terms, when you say “35%,” you mean your federal marginal bracket, not like your effective rate, right?
Client: Yes, marginal. The top bracket I hit.

Client: January 4th, 1961.
Advisor: January 4th, 1961—thank you.
Advisor: Quick pause—when you said earlier the Social Security number was gross, you meant before taxes and Medicare premiums, right?

Advisor: That totals about $1,358,400 in investable assets. Does that match what you see on your end?
Client: Yes—$1,358,400 is the total I’ve got written down.
Advisor: Great. Now, separate from investable assets—your home. What’s the approximate value of your primary residence?
Client: About $419,600.
Advisor: And any loan outstanding on the home—mortgage, HELOC—anything at all?

Client: Sure.
Advisor: Right now, are you working at all, even part-time, or would you say you’re not employed at the moment?
Client: I’m not working right now—so, unemployed, I guess.
Advisor: Got it. When you say “unemployed,” do you mean actively job-hunting, or more like retired/not working by choice?
Client: More like not working by choice. I’m not out there applying places.

Client: More like not working by choice. I’m not out there applying places.
Advisor: Okay, helpful—so not employed currently. And just so I don’t label it wrong: what kind of work did you do most of your career, broadly?
Client: Operations. Like, operations management stuff.
Advisor: Operations—thank you. Was that mostly in one industry, or it bounced around?
Client: A couple industries, but the role was always operations.

Client: It’s basically Social Security. I think it’s like, 190-ish a year? That sounds high when I say it out loud.

Advisor: And is that income coming in like clockwork monthly, or is it irregular—like it shows up at different times or in chunks?
Client: It’s not like a paycheck. It’s kind of… not consistent.
Advisor: Okay, so more ad hoc—irregular timing—rather than a fixed monthly deposit?
Client: Yes, ad hoc is a good word for it.
Advisor: Great. And just to be super clear on ownership—this Social Security income is yours, Emily, not tied to anyone else’s benefit coming in separately.

Advisor: Okay. Now, I’m going to ask a slightly weird admin-y question, Emily, and you can correct me if I’m off.
Client: Okay…
Advisor: In our notes, we label people as Client 1, Client 2, that kind of thing. Since it’s just you, you’re Client 1, correct?
Client: Yes—Client 1.
Advisor: Wait—sorry, I might be mixing labels. You’re the only adult, so you’re the primary person on the household, and we call you Client 1. That’s what you mean too?
